title: Protocol for fabricating elastomeric stencils for patterned stem cell differentiation
author: Lehr, Merrin, et al.
description: SummaryGeometrically controlled stem cell differentiation promotes reproducible pattern formation. Here, we present a protocol to fabricate elastomeric stencils for patterned stem cell differentiation. We describe procedures for using photolithography to produce molds, followed by molding polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) to obtain stencils with through holes. We then provide instructions for culturing cells on stencils and, finally, removing stencils to allow colony growth and cell migration. This approach yields reproducible two-dimensional organoids tailored for quantitative studies of growth and pattern formation.For complete details on the use and execution of this protocol, please refer to Lehr etÂ al.1

steps_raw.title: Preparation of mouse embryonic stem cells
Preparation of mouse embryonic stem cells
steps_raw.instruction: This step describes the preparation of mouse ESCs to use for differentiation. Cells need to be thawed at least 1Â week before using them for differentiation; therefore, this step must be completed in advance.5.Coat a dish with 0.1% gelatin for at least 20Â min.Note: Choose an appropriate dish with a surface area that matches the cell number. We freeze 500,000 cells per tube and thaw them onto a 60Â mm Cellbind dish.6.Thaw a frozen stock of ESCs.a.Resuspend the ESCs in 10Â mL warm N2B27 medium.b.Centrifuge at 218Â Ă—Â g for 4Â min using a benchtop centrifuge.c.Resuspend the pellet in N2B27Â + 2iÂ + LIF medium and plate cells on the coated culture dish.7.Incubate at 37Â°C, 5% CO2.Note: Provide fresh N2B27Â + 2iÂ + LIF daily.8.Split cells once ESCs form numerous compact colonies with a clear boundary (usually after 2â€“3Â days).Note: Prevent overgrowth. When mESCs begin to touch and merge, they will spontaneously differentiate and not perform well in the differentiation assay.9.Maintain cells in N2B27Â + 2iÂ + LIF medium for at least 1Â week before using them for the differentiation assay.

steps_raw.title: Part 1: Mold preparation
steps_raw.instruction: For mold fabrication, a silicon wafer coated with photoresist (SU8) is exposed to UV light through a photomask to activate the SU8 in a specific pattern. Subsequent baking solidifies the pattern, and unexposed regions are dissolved in the developer to leave the desired mold pattern. The mold is then coated with a hydrophobic silane layer to prevent unwanted adhesion of PDMS during stencil production.Note: There are numerous steps. Familiarize yourself with the workflow.Note: For experienced users, the mold preparation step should be highly reproducible. Difficulties that users may encounter are covered in the troubleshooting section.
